answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent trauma or injury to your back or ribs?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Any shortness of breath, cough, or chest pain along with the back pain?
answers.intake_q6: Any numbness, weakness, or tingling in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a7: I tried tylenol but it didn't help very much
answers.intake_q8: Any history of kidney problems, recent urinary symptoms, or abdominal pain?
answers.intake_q9: Any history of osteoporosis, cancer, or recent infections?
answers.intake_q10: Can you describe the nature of the pain? For example, is it sharp, dull, burning, or something else?
evidence_search_queries: adult back pain treatment dosing acetaminophen mg duration contraindications
back pain red flags and return precautions adult

note.soap:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of dull aching pain localized to the left side of the lower rib cage. She denies any recent trauma, fever, chills, weight loss, respiratory symptoms, numbness, weakness, or urinary complaints. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) without significant relief. There is no history of osteoporosis, cancer, or recent infections. O: Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal pain likely related to a strain or minor soft tissue injury localized to the left lower rib cage area. Differential:
1. Musculoskeletal strain or intercostal muscle irritation
2. Costochondritis or rib inflammation
3. Herpes zoster (early stage without rash yet)
4. Referred pain from visceral causes such as splenic or renal pathology (less likely given no systemic symptoms)

P:
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid NSAIDs if history of renal or liver disease; acetaminophen should not exceed 3000 mg per day
 - Alternatives: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen if no contraindications
 - Acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hours as needed for pain (use caution in liver disease; consider lower max daily dose)
 - Return precautions: Advise patient to seek care if she develops fever, worsening pain, numbness, weakness, rash, or new systemic symptoms
 - Testing: Not indicated at this time given absence of red flags or systemic symptoms; consider imaging or labs if symptoms worsen or fail to improve after conservative management

evidence.title: WHO releases guidelines on chronic low back pain
evidence.snippet: The World Health Organization (WHO) is releasing its first-ever guidelines on managing chronic low back pain (LBP) in primary and community care settings, listing interventions for health workers to use and also to not use during routine care.

evidence.title: Management of Low Back Pain: Guidelines From the VA/DoD | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Low back pain is one of the most frequently experienced medical conditions, affecting 84% of adults in the United States at some point. Nearly 40% of adults will have experienced this type of pain within a three-month period. Low back pain is the leading cause of disability worldwide. The U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs and U.S. Department of Defense (VA/DoD) have published guidelines on the diagnosis and treatment of low back pain.

evidence.title: Noninvasive Treatments for Acute, Subacute, and Chronic Low Back Pain: A Clinical Practice Guideline From the American College of Physicians
evidence.snippet: In patients with chronic low back pain who have had an inadequate response to nonpharmacologic therapy, clinicians and patients should consider pharmacologic treatment with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs as first-line therapy, or tramadol or duloxetine as second-line therapy.
